name: troubleshooting
author: dojo-community
context: Diagnose stale indexes, held-back packages, repository trust failures, broken state, and APT lock contention
info: This guide groups the most common real-world APT failure modes into actionable categories: stale metadata, dependency breakage, repository authentication errors, packages kept back, and lock contention from concurrent package operations. It points agents toward the right node rather than encouraging blind retries or lock-file deletion. Use it when package work fails with confusing solver output, when a repository stops authenticating, or when a host behaves differently than the source definitions suggest.

sections.title: Stale or missing indexes
sections.body: If package discovery or version selection looks wrong, refresh indexes first. APT cache inspection is only as current as the last successful `apt-get update`.

sections.title: Kept-back packages
sections.body: Packages kept back during `apt upgrade` are often normal on Ubuntu because of phased updates. Security updates are not phased. Use `apt policy <package>` and [[apt/upgrade#held-back]] before treating kept-back output as a repair issue.

sections.title: Repository authentication failures
sections.body: Unsigned archives, missing Release files, or incorrect `Signed-By` paths break repository trust. The right fix is to correct the source definition or keyring, not to disable security checks globally. See [[apt/repositories#keyrings]].

sections.title: Lock contention
sections.body: Lock errors mean another package operation may still be running. Identify the owner with `lsof` or `fuser`, determine whether it is expected, and retry only after the competing process exits or is intentionally stopped.

links.context: Repair broken dependencies and inspect lock owners
Understand upgrade behavior that is not actually a failure
Explain source precedence and candidate selection
Investigate preference rules that may be holding a version back

related.note: Repair is the executable follow-up for broken package state and lock inspection

body:
# Troubleshooting APT Failures

Treat APT failures as one of a few categories instead of a single generic problem.

## 1. Stale or missing metadata

- Symptom: package not found, old version table, or candidate does not match expected repositories.
- First action: `sudo apt-get update`
- Next action: inspect with [[apt/search#policy]]

APT answers inspection questions from the local cache, not directly from the network. Many "wrong version" problems are simply stale indexes.

## 2. Packages kept back

On Ubuntu, kept-back packages during `apt upgrade` are often the result of phased updates and do not require repair. Check [[apt/upgrade#held-back]] before forcing a package install. If the package is not merely phased, inspect source precedence in [[apt/pinning]].

## 3. Broken package state

- Symptom: unmet dependencies, half-configured packages, interrupted install
- First action: `sudo apt-get check`
- Follow-up: [[apt/repair#configure-pending]] and [[apt/repair#fix-broken]]

## 4. Repository authentication failures

- Symptom: unsigned repository, missing `Release` file, `Signed-By` mismatch
- First action: audit the source definition and keyring path in [[apt/repositories#keyrings]]
- Important note: Debian documents insecure repositories as strongly discouraged and likely to lose compatibility over time

## 5. Lock contention

- Symptom: `Could not get lock /var/lib/dpkg/lock-frontend`
- First action: inspect the owning process via [[apt/repair#locks]]
- Do not: delete lock files blindly while another package operation is active

## Logs

Ubuntu documents package installation and removal activity in `/var/log/dpkg.log`. Use it to confirm what happened before you try to replay or undo a failed transaction.
